channel.title: NPR Topics: Science
channel.description: The latest health and science news. Updates on medicine, healthy living, nutrition, drugs, diet, and advances in science and technology. Subscribe to the Health & Science podcast.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: In cities, wild things are hiding everywhere — if you put on your 'nature eyes'
item.description: NPR's Ailsa Chang talks with Amy Jaecker-Jones of the Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County about a worldwide community science project happening this weekend — the City Nature Challenge.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Ailsa Chang talks with Amy Jaecker-Jones of the Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County about a worldwide community science project happening this weekend — the City Nature Challenge.
item.dc:creator: Gurjit Kaur

item.title: AI's a suck up. Research shows how it flatters and suggests we're not to blame
item.description: The AI models and chatbots that we interact with tend to affirm our feelings and viewpoints — more so than people do, with potentially worrisome consequences.
item.content:encoded: The AI models and chatbots that we interact with tend to affirm our feelings and viewpoints — more so than people do, with potentially worrisome consequences.
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: Instead of civil war, a naked mole rat colony changed queens peacefully
item.description: These matriarchal rodents often have bloody succession wars to replace their queen. But in a colony in California, Queen Tere ceded the throne to her daughter, Arwen, without violence.
item.content:encoded:
These matriarchal rodents often have bloody succession wars to replace their queen. But in a colony in California, Queen Tere ceded the throne to her daughter, Arwen, without violence.

(Image credit: Evgeniya Moskova/iStockphoto)
item.dc:creator: Pien Huang

item.title: A mine despoiled the beauty of the rainforest. This Goldman Prize winner took action
item.description: "We women are the land guardians and keepers," says Theonila Roka Matbob of Papua New Guinea, recognized for her efforts to repair the environmental and social harms caused by a copper and gold mine.
item.content:encoded:
"We women are the land guardians and keepers," says Theonila Roka Matbob of Papua New Guinea, recognized for her efforts to repair the environmental and social harms caused by a copper and gold mine.

(Image credit: Goldman Environmental Prize)
item.dc:creator: Gabrielle Emanuel

item.title: The Little Probe That Could: Why Voyager 1 Matters, and Why NASA Just Switched Part of It Off
item.description: This week, NASA announced it had shut down one of that spacecraft's remaining science instruments — not because the mission has failed, but to keep it alive a little longer.
item.content:encoded:
This week, NASA announced it had shut down one of that spacecraft's remaining science instruments — not because the mission has failed, but to keep it alive a little longer.

(Image credit: NASA)
item.dc:creator: Willem Marx

item.title: Photos: How overfishing in Southeast Asia is an ecological and human crisis
item.description: A rare look at one of the world's most critical and understudied environmental crises. Southeast Asia produces more than half of the world's fish, yet its waters are among the most depleted and contested.
item.content:encoded:
A rare look at one of the world's most critical and understudied environmental crises. Southeast Asia produces more than half of the world's fish, yet its waters are among the most depleted and contested.

(Image credit: Nicole Tung)
item.dc:creator: Nicole Tung

item.title: How a Japanese poet's diary helps scientists reconstruct solar cycles
item.description: Researchers used a Japanese poet's diary to track solar events that took place over 800 years ago.
item.content:encoded: Researchers used a Japanese poet's diary to track solar events that took place over 800 years ago.
item.dc:creator: Scott Simon

item.title: Animal activists celebrate their first global 'Sanctuary Day'
item.description: It's been 40 years since animal advocates founded a sanctuary for farm animals in New York and California, and they say April 17 is their first global sanctuary day.
item.content:encoded: It's been 40 years since animal advocates founded a sanctuary for farm animals in New York and California, and they say April 17 is their first global sanctuary day.
item.dc:creator: Abigail Bottar

item.title: This week in science: Small talk, more human lobster killing, and an ancient flood
item.description: NPR's Short Wave team talks about the surprising benefits of small talk, more humane ways to kill lobsters, and an ancient flood that may have helped create the Grand Canyon.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Short Wave team talks about the surprising benefits of small talk, more humane ways to kill lobsters, and an ancient flood that may have helped create the Grand Canyon.
item.dc:creator: Emily Kwong

item.title: How seals' whiskers make them master underwater hunters
item.description: Their sensitive facial hair may be the harbor seals superpower for tracking fish, scientists are learning.
item.content:encoded:
Their sensitive facial hair may be the harbor seals superpower for tracking fish, scientists are learning.

(Image credit: Robin Heinrich/Marine Science Center)
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel
